items.headline: The Point Of Building Online
items.angle: Connect the feeling of being truly 'offline' and present directly to the intentional work of building an online business that enables it. Frame the adventure not as an escape, but as the entire purpose of the work.
hooks: This is the entire reason we build things online.
The whole point of building an online business isn't the business itself.
They sell the hustle. I'm interested in what comes after.
There is nothing quite like a Tuesday afternoon with zero calls on the calendar.
What if your business was designed to be forgotten for a few hours?
talking_points: The whole point of building an online business isn't actually the business.
It's about moments like this. It's about being fully present, with no notifications, no client fires, no content to post.
It's about a quiet Tuesday afternoon where your time is actually your own.
Most people accidentally build themselves a digital prison. A business that owns them instead of the other way around.
But you can choose to build differently. You can build engines, not just a job.
You can use automation and AI to handle the repetitive work, so you don't have to.
It allows you to create a business that funds your life without consuming it.
This is what we mean when we say 'Build Online, Live Offline'.
It’s not a fantasy. It is a design choice.
The goal is to get back to the field, whatever your field may be.
items.body_copy: The whole point of building an online business is not the business itself. It is about the quiet moments. It is about being fully present, with no notifications pinging, no client fires to put out, no urgent emails in your inbox. It is about a random afternoon where your time is completely your own. So many founders I know accidentally build themselves a digital prison. They create a business that owns them, not the other way around. But you can make a different choice. You can build systems and engines that do the work for you. You can leverage smart automation and AI to buy back your time, so you can spend it out here. This is the core idea of FieldWork. To build a business that funds your life without consuming it. Build Online. Live Offline. It is not a fantasy. It is a design choice. The whole goal is to return to the field, whatever your field happens to be.
script: Hook: The real point of an online business is offline freedom.
Problem: Most founders build a business that traps them.
Solution: Intentionally design your business to serve your life.
Mechanism: Use automation and AI to create systems that run for you.
Payoff: Reclaim your time for moments of presence and adventure.
Philosophy: This is the essence of 'Build Online, Live Offline'.
guidance: Film the opening talking points (A-roll) in a natural, 'offline' setting like sitting by a lake, on a porch, or next to a campfire.

Use beautiful, cinematic B-roll footage for the middle section of the video. Show examples of your own 'offline' life: hiking, time with family, exploring, etc. This mirrors the visual appeal of the original reel.

End on a powerful, scenic shot with the words 'Build Online. Live Offline.' appearing on screen. This is a brand awareness play, not a direct response CTA.

items.headline: How I Slept Through a Business Crisis
items.angle: Frame a common business 'disaster,' like a key system failing or a major client leaving, as the 'earthquake.' Then, reveal how the automated, resilient systems built through FieldWork allowed you to remain calm and 'offline,' mirroring the original's structure of a huge problem being neutralized by a specific focus.
hooks: Our main ad account just got shut down. Here’s why I didn’t care.
My operations manager called me in a panic. I told him I was going for a hike.
Everyone thinks you need to hustle when a crisis hits. They are wrong.
The system I built that makes business emergencies feel boring.
Our biggest client churned last Tuesday. My response? I made a cup of coffee.
talking_points: A few weeks ago, our primary lead generation system completely failed overnight. No new leads were coming in. Zero.
A couple of years ago, this would have meant canceling my entire week. It would have been all-nighters on Zoom calls and a massive spike of stress.
But my first thought this time was not panic. It was, 'Huh, that's interesting'. Then I went and finished my morning coffee.
The reason for that calm is that we built the business to be resilient from the ground up.
We do not rely on a single channel. We have automated backup systems that were already kicking in to nurture our existing audience and open up other pathways.
This is the entire philosophy behind FieldWork. You build the engines once, and you build them right, so they can handle the inevitable shocks that come with running a business.
It is not about avoiding problems, because you can't. It is about building a business that protects your time and your peace, so you can stay in the field, living your life.
If you want to see how we design these kinds of shock-proof systems for founders and experts, I put together a free workshop.

items.headline: How I Fired Myself From My Own Business
items.angle: Frame the story around a specific moment of burnout where you realized your 'successful' business was actually a cage. The villain isn't a person, but the hustle culture itself, and the solution is the FieldWork philosophy of building systems to reclaim your life.
hooks: I had a successful business, and I had to burn it to the ground.
The most important meeting I ever missed was with my own family.
My business was making good money, but it was costing me my life.
I replaced myself as CEO with a handful of simple automations.
Here’s why I walked away from the business everyone told me to build.
talking_points: It was a Tuesday afternoon in spring, and I was on a Zoom call closing a big deal.
Through the window behind my screen, I could see my kids playing in the yard. And I just felt this huge disconnect, like I was watching my own life on a screen.
I had built exactly what I thought I wanted. A business that was growing fast. But it was a trap. It needed me, personally, for every single important decision and every fire.
I was working harder than ever just to keep the whole thing from falling apart. I was constantly available but never truly present.
Faced with that, I had two choices: I could keep chasing the next level of growth and accept this as the price of success.
Or, I could tear the whole thing down to its foundations and rebuild it to serve my life, not the other way around.
So I did. I started building systems. I automated everything I could. I intentionally stepped back, and it was terrifying. I had to trust the engines I was building.
And it worked. The business runs better now, and I got my time back. I got my presence back.
This transformation came down to two things: letting go of the ego that said I had to do everything, and embracing simple systems that could do the work for me.
If you feel like you're building a prison instead of a business, I put the framework I used into a guide. Just comment the word 'FIELD' below and I'll send it over to you.

Faced with that, I had two choices. I could keep chasing the next level of growth and accept this as the price. Or, I could tear it down and rebuild it to serve my life, not the other way around. I chose to rebuild.

Think of it as building a sales team, a marketing team, and an operations team that are all code. They do not get tired and they do not take holidays.
This is how you stop being the operator and start being the owner again. It is how you get back to the field.
I put together a free workshop showing how these engines work.

We teach our clients in FieldWork how to build that boat. A business that runs itself so you can get back to your life.

The 'live offline' version of you is still there. If you want the plan to unbury that version of you, comment 'FieldWork' below and I will send you the details.
script: Hook: This was me before my business trapped me.
Problem: We normalize this trap as 'paying dues'.
Reframe: The truth is, we buried our original 'why'.
Lost Ideals: The freedom and life we originally wanted.
Trigger: Seeing others live freely is not envy, it's a reminder.
Punchline: Your 'live offline' self is still there.
CTA: Comment 'FieldWork' for the plan to get it back.
guidance: Film this as a talking head in a warm, natural setting, like outdoors or a simple room with natural light.
Use B-roll of you living 'offline': hiking, by the water, with family. Use an old photo for the opening line if possible.
On-screen text should emphasize key phrases: 'Before your business owned you', 'That's not envy.', 'It's a reminder.'
Music should be thoughtful and slightly nostalgic, not an upbeat trending audio.
End with a clear, simple text overlay: Comment 'FieldWork'

items.headline: The Business You Need to Escape From
items.angle: Frame the common entrepreneurial goal of 'freedom' as a trap. Christian should speak from experience about building a business that became a prison, and share the mindset shift required to build one that actually serves your life.
hooks: Stop building a business you need to escape from.
The hustle is a trap. Take it from me.
Your business should fund your life, not become it.
I built a prison and called it a business.
You don't want what you think you want.
talking_points: Stop building a business you need to escape from.
So many of us start a business with the dream of freedom, more time, more travel, more life.
But then we get a few years in and realize we have just built ourselves a more demanding, 24/7 job with a much tougher boss.
We build these complex machines that depend entirely on us. The machine doesn't run unless we do.
The goal isn't to build something you can one day sell or escape. The goal is to build something that gives you your life back, starting now.
It's about using systems, automation, and AI to build smart engines that do the work for you.
This is how you build your business online, so you can actually live your life offline.
I put together a free workshop that walks you through the framework for building these engines.
If you want access, just comment the word SYSTEMS below and I will send it right over.
items.body_copy: Stop building a business you need to escape from. So many of us start a business with the dream of freedom. More time, more travel, more life. script: Hook: State the core problem of building a business-prison.
Empathize with the common but flawed entrepreneurial dream.
Describe the reality of becoming trapped by your own creation.
Reframe the goal: Build for your life now, not for a future escape.
Introduce the solution: Systems and automation.
Connect the solution to the 'Build Online, Live Offline' philosophy.
CTA: Offer a free workshop with a single word comment.
guidance: Shoot this as a direct to camera talking head. Maintain steady, warm eye contact. A simple, natural background works best.
